children.name: Pipa
children.description: Pipa — a genus of clawed frogs in the family Pipidae.

children.name: Pipa arrabali
children.description: The Arrabal's Suriname toad is a species of frog in the family Pipidae found in Brazil, Guyana, Suriname, Venezuela, and possibly Peru. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests, freshwater marshes, intermittent freshwater marshes, ponds, and canals and ditches. It is threatened by habitat loss.

children.name: Pipa aspera
children.description: Pipa aspera, the Albina Surinam toad, is a species of frog in the family Pipidae found in French Guiana, Suriname, and possibly Brazil.

children.name: Pipa carvalhoi
children.description: Carvalho's Surinam toad is a species of frog in the family Pipidae endemic to Brazil. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical dry forests, dry savanna, moist savanna, subtropical or tropical dry shrubland, subtropical or tropical moist shrubland, freshwater marshes, ponds, and aquaculture ponds. It is threatened by habitat loss.

children.name: Pipa myersi
children.description: Myers' Surinam toad is a species of frog in the family Pipidae found in Panama and possibly Colombia. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests and rivers. It is threatened by habitat loss.

children.name: Pipa parva
children.description: The Sabana Surinam toad (Pipa parva), also known as the dwarf toad, is a species of frog in the family Pipidae. It is found in northwestern Venezuela and northeastern Colombia (Norte de Santander and La Guajira Departments), mainly in the Maracaibo Basin. There is an introduced (and expanding) population in the Lake Valencia Basin in northern Venezuela.

children.name: Pipa pipa
children.description: The common Surinam toad, the Suriname toad, or star-fingered toad, is a fully-aquatic species of frog, in the family Pipidae, with a widespread range across much of tropical South America and the island of Trinidad. The females of this species are well-known for "incubating" their eggs on their backs, in honeycomb-like depressions directly within the skin, releasing fully-formed froglets after a period of 4–5 months. Pipa pipa is an ambush predator, lying in-wait underwater for prey to inevitably wander too close, swiftly inhaling the unsuspecting creature using suction feeding.

children.name: Pipa snethlageae
children.description: Pipa snethlageae, the Utinga Surinam toad, is a species of frog in the family Pipidae found in Brazil, Colombia, Peru, and possibly Venezuela.

children.name: Hymenochirus curtipes

children.name: Hymenochirus feae

children.name: Xenopus allofraseri
children.description: {{Speciesbox|image=|status=LC|status_system=IUCN3.1|status_ref= |taxon=Xenopus allofraseri|authority=Evans, Carter, Greenbaum, Gvozdík, Kelley, McLaughlin, Pauwels, Portik, Stanley et al., 2015 It inhabits wetlands and forests in countries such as the Democratic Republic of Congo, Guinea and Angola.

children.name: Xenopus arabiensis
children.description: Xenopus arabiensis — a species in the genus Xenopus, family Pipidae. Also known as clawed frogs.

children.name: Xenopus boumbaensis
children.description: Xenopus boumbaensis, the Mawa clawed frog, is a predominantly to fully aquatic species of frog in the family Pipidae, known from a few localities in central and southern Cameroon, the northwestern Republic of the Congo and the extreme southwest of the Central African Republic.

children.name: Xenopus calcaratus
children.description: {{Speciesbox|image=Xenopus calcaratus 1.jpg|status=dd|status_system=IUCN3.1|status_ref= |taxon=Xenopus calcaratus|authority=Peters, 1875.

children.name: Xenopus clivii
children.description: Category:Taxa named by Mario Giacinto Peracca.

children.name: Xenopus eysoole

children.name: Xenopus fischbergi
children.description: {{Speciesbox|status=LC|status_system=IUCN3.1|status_ref= |taxon=Xenopus fischbergi|authority=Evans, Carter, Greenbaum, Gvozdík, Kelley, McLaughlin, Pauwels, Portik, Stanley et al., 2015 It occurs in habitats such as savannas and wetlands.

children.name: Xenopus itombwensis
children.description: Xenopus itombwensis is a species of frog in the family Pipidae endemic to the Itombwe Massif of the South Kivu Province of the Democratic Republic of the Congo.

children.description: {{Speciesbox|status=DD|status_system=IUCN3.1|status_ref= |taxon=Xenopus kobeli|authority=Evans, Carter, Greenbaum, Gvoždík, Kelley, McLaughlin, Pauwels, Portik, Stanley, Tinsley, Tobias, & Blackburn, 2015.

children.name: Xenopus laevis
children.description: Category:Taxa named by François Marie Daudin Category:Amphibians described in 1802.

children.name: Xenopus lenduensis
children.description: Xenopus lenduensis, the Lendu Plateau clawed frog, is a species of frog in the family Pipidae endemic to the Orientale Province of the Democratic Republic of the Congo.

children.name: Xenopus mellotropicalis
children.description: {{Speciesbox|status=LC|image=Xenopus mellotropicalis.jpg|status_system=IUCN3.1|status_ref= |taxon=Xenopus mellotropicalis|authority=Evans, Carter, Greenbaum, Gvozdík, Kelley, McLaughlin, Pauwels, Portik, Stanley et al., 2015 It is listed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List and populations are currently stable.

children.name: Xenopus parafraseri
children.description: Xenopus parafraseri, the upland clawed frog, is an endemic species of frog in Gabon, Congo, and Cameroon in Central and West Africa.

children.name: Xenopus poweri
children.description: {{Speciesbox|status=LC|status_system=IUCN3.1|status_ref= |taxon=Xenopus poweri|authority=Hewitt, 1927 found in shrublands, wetlands, forests, savannas and grasslands of Central and Southern Africa.
